label: Divergent tetA/tetR control region: TetR-repressible / aTc-inducible.
MBP is an uncommonly effective solubility-enhancing fusion partner; the solubility enhancement is an intrinsic property of the MBP fold
Solubility enhancement is an intrinsic property of MBP, but folding of the passenger is either spontaneous or chaperone-mediated
MBP binds amylose resin, enabling one-step affinity purification of fusions; bound protein is eluted with maltose
Native ligands are maltose and linear maltodextrins; ligand binding underlies the amylose-resin affinity handle
Tag is removed after purification by site-specific protease cleavage at a recognition site engineered into the linker (e.g. Factor Xa)
MBP fusions accumulate to a high level in E. coli (on the order of a few percent of total cellular protein)
Native to and primarily used in Escherichia coli
Passenger sequences can promote interlaced (domain-swapped) dimers in a common MBP variant — a caution for structural studies of MBP fusions
A surface-entropy-reduced engineered variant (MBPeng) carrying ~8 substitutions is widely used to improve crystallizability of MBP fusions

Adjacent histidine residues chelate immobilized divalent transition-metal ions (Ni2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Zn2+) on an IMAC matrix, enabling single-step affinity purification; protein is eluted by imidazole competition or low pH.
Genetic fusion of the polyhistidine peptide to a recombinant protein permits one-step IMAC purification of the fusion product.
Host- and organism-agnostic: as a small synthetic peptide the tag functions across expression hosts and works under both native and denaturing (e.g. 6–8 M urea / guanidine) conditions.
Common length variants of the polyhistidine tag exist (e.g. 8xHis, 10xHis); the 6xHis form (HHHHHH) is the canonical minimal tag.
FLAG is an engineered hydrophilic 8-aa epitope/affinity tag for detection and purification of recombinant proteins
FLAG is recognized by the anti-FLAG monoclonal antibodies M1 (Ca2+-dependent, N-terminal-specific), M2 (Ca2+-independent, position-insensitive) and M5
The C-terminal DDDDK of FLAG is an enterokinase (enteropeptidase) recognition site, allowing proteolytic removal of an N-terminal FLAG tag
The minimal anti-FLAG M2 epitope is DYK(D)/DYKxxD; residues D5 and D7 are dispensable for M2 binding
In the secretory pathway, Tyr2 of FLAG can be sulfated by TPST2 (SLC35B2-dependent), reducing anti-FLAG M2 and polyclonal detection of secreted/membrane proteins
LacI-controllable T7 phi10 promoter (requires T7 RNA polymerase).
TetR repression at the tetO1 operator is relieved by tetracycline / anhydrotetracycline (aTc).
